author: manondorf
text:
Wisconsinite here, it's shockingly prevalent and normalized. Of the 50 drunkest counties in the country, [41 are in Wisconsin](https://vinepair.com/booze-news/map-drunkest-and-driest-counties-america/). We top the leaderboards for [specific cities](https://www.datapandas.org/ranking/drunkest-cities-in-america), too.

The map in that first link shows a stark contrast between us and neighboring states that get as little sunlight and as much snow as we do, so I can't blame it all on that. It's like people take pride in how regularly and how much they drink.

author: FraggleBiologist
text:
Does this not terrify anyone else? How long is our brain picking up enough information from the outside world for us to process? Do we even process them? It would be easy to check for full brain death before rolling patients to a morgue, but those bodies at murder scenes, are they still processing anything?

We don't know if they are good memories. We don't really know what it is, it's just a lot nicer to believe you remember all those wonderful moments before our brain shuts down.

author: Crazy-Gate-948
text:
I remember reading that there were actually coordinates left at the site pointing to some obscure book about Aboriginal hunting techniques. The whole thing feels like an elaborate art project that got way out of hand.

What gets me is the sheer scale - you'd need serious equipment and planning to pull this off without anyone noticing. Plus the GPS precision in 1998 wasn't exactly consumer grade stuff.

The fact that it's still visible after all these years is wild too. Most land art disappears pretty quickly but the outback conditions must be perfect for preservation. I wonder if whoever made it checks satellite images sometimes just to see their work still there.

author: TrueInDueTime
text:
I (32M) tried so hard in 2025 to get a girlfriend. Singles mixers, speed dating events, dating apps, trying to ask friends if they had any single friends, doing a bunch of different social events to meet people, talking about being single on social media (and sometimes having vids that blew up, like 20K - 200K views). 

Nothing worked out. I went on one date this year. Early January. With a woman who had DMed me on TikTok. That ended up not working out. 

So now I'm just chilling. I have a great life without the romance. There are so many things that are going well in my life. At this point I don't care if I meet someone or not. Because I have learned to be happy while being single. 

It's also peace of mind knowing that I have tried harder than most people to find someone to date, and since it still hasn't worked out, I won't have regrets.

author: TrueInDueTime
text:
I feel like, back in college (for me 2011-2015), there were these two metaphorical roads that people were starting to travel on. 

One road was for the people who were getting into relationships, hooking up, having sex, and learning how to initiate a sexual relationship.

The other road was for the people who were not going to parties, not interested in dating, or were going to parties but not able to develop conversations with people they were sexually interested in. I was on this road. 

And the two roads diverged, and now, at 32, it's EXTREMELY difficult for me to somehow hack my way through the woods to get to the other road.

author: ManicMakerStudios
text:
If you sleep 8 hours/day and are awake for 16, that 112 hours/week that you have every single week to live your life.  Some of that time will be taken up by daily necessities, like hygiene, preparing and eating meals, cleaning/laundry etc.

If you set aside 40 hours out of that week for work, that's 72 hours left for everything else.  That means nearly 2/3 of your waking life is "you time".

When you put it that way, it seems kind of decadent, doesn't it?

author: Billy1121
text:
For context, Medicaid pays for longer nursing home stays while Medicare does not. So many seniors have to go on Medicaid in their later years to afford long term nursing home care.

Different states go about it in different ways. This article refers to Iowa which uses a third party vendor who gets 11% of all recoveries, so they recovered about $29 million in 2019. Whereas Hawaii has about half the population of Iowa and only recovered around $30,000.

Depending on the state, retirement accounts or homes may be included or excluded. Iowa is brutal because in this article they even went after back pay because the daughter of an elderly person unwittingly enrolled in a program to pay caregivers, which was (unknown to her) actually a Medicaid program.

> "There's a discrimination against what I call 'the wrong type of disease,'" Einhart says. Medicare could spend hundreds of thousands of dollars on hospital treatment for a person with serious heart problems or cancer, and no government representatives would try to recoup the money from the person's estate. But people with other conditions, such as dementia, often need extended nursing home care, which Medicare won't cover. Many such patients wind up on Medicaid, and their estates are billed.

author: Mlakeside
text:
Considering elks live natively in Eurasia, in addition to North America and "moose" being a loanword Native American Algonquin language, it would be weird if we didn't have a word for the animal until post-Columbian times.


The reason why moose replaced elk in English is simply because Britain doesn't have them, so when English settlers first saw an elk in America, they didn't have (or rather, didn't know they had) a word for the animal. This is also likely the reason they named the wapiti "elk", despite it not looking like one: they might have known an elk was a type of large deer, so they just used the term they had.
